title: Gâteau Basque
subtitle: The dense Basque butter cake filled with black cherry jam
excerpt: A thick, rich Basque cake made from a sweet almond-scented dough pressed into a tin and filled with black cherry jam. More pastry than cake in texture: dense, buttery, and fragrant, baked until deep golden and crisp on the surface.
sourceNotes: Gâteau Basque is documented as a traditional Basque Country pastry from the border region of France and Spain. The recipe appears in French regional cookery literature and in Elizabeth David, French Provincial Cooking (1960), as a characteristic pastry of the Pays Basque. The traditional fillings are either black cherry jam (confiture de cerises noires) or pastry cream; both are historically attested. The recipe is public domain.

content.text: Gâteau Basque is made from a thick

pressed into a tin in two layers (base and lid) around a filling of black cherry jam. It bakes to a deep golden crust with a characteristic crosshatch pattern scored across the top. The inside is dense and buttery, somewhere between a pastry and a cake, very different from the light crumb of a sponge.

The dough uses ground almonds alongside plain flour, which contributes to the characteristic slightly sandy crumb. The dough is too soft to roll conventionally; chill it well before working with it, and press or pat it into the tin rather than trying to roll a cold slab.

attrs.prepNote: black cherry; or use a good sour cherry jam

content.text: Beat {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} together until pale and combined. Beat in {{eggs}} one at a time, then add {{vanilla-extract}} and the zest from {{lemon}}. Fold in {{plain-flour}}, {{ground-almonds}}, and {{baking-powder}} until a soft dough forms. Divide into two equal pieces, wrap both in cling film, and refrigerate for 60 minutes.

Heat the oven to 175°C fan (195°C conventional). Grease a 22 cm loose-bottomed cake tin and line the base with baking paper. Press one portion of dough evenly across the base and up the sides of the tin to about 1 cm depth. Spread {{cherry-jam}} across the base, leaving a 1 cm border. Pat or roll the second portion of dough into a round and lay it over the jam to form a lid; press the edges to seal. Beat {{egg-yolks}} with a teaspoon of water and brush over the top. Use the tines of a fork to score a crosshatch pattern across the surface. Bake for 45 to 50 minutes until deep golden on top and firm to the touch; a skewer inserted into the dough layer at the edge should come out clean. Cool in the tin for 30 minutes before turning out.
The gâteau is eaten at room temperature, not warm. Cut into wedges with a sharp knife. It slices cleanly when cooled.
Pastry cream is the other traditional filling. Spread a layer of thick vanilla custard (about 300 g) in place of the jam.
A dense cake is correct. If the centre feels undercooked after 50 minutes, add another 5 to 10 minutes: the filling insulates the centre and it takes longer than expected.
